title: How to Use Menhausen
preview: Five minutes a day to stay steady—no awkward talking required.
content:
If you opened this, you’ve already done more than most men. A lot of guys ages 25–45 never talk to anyone about what’s going on—not because “everything’s fine,” but because “real men handle it” and they’re always “good.” We built Menhausen so “I’m fine” can actually mean your head is in order—not “I can’t say what I feel.” We get it. We’ve been there too.

## What Menhausen really is
Menhausen is a Telegram Mini App for men 25–45.

**Fully anonymous by design:** your data lives on your device. No names, no emails, no sign-ups. Open it in Telegram and you’re in.

This isn’t therapy and it’s not a heart-to-heart. It’s a daily self-support ritual built on evidence-based methods:

- **CBT** — spot and rewrite automatic thoughts that push you toward burnout.
- **ACT** — make room for emotions and still take action.
- **Mindfulness (MBSR-style)** — awareness without candles and “be present” clichés.

Research backs this up: short daily practices can reduce anxiety and burnout symptoms in as little as 7–14 days. We packaged it into a format that fits real life—deadlines, family pressure, fear of failure, and that low-grade irritation with coworkers.

## Let’s kill the main myths

- **“A real man handles it alone.”** Sure—but not empty-handed. Real men use tools. You don’t “tough it out” when your car breaks down; you fix it. Same with your head.
- **“Emotions are weakness.”** No. Emotions are data. A 10‑second mood check‑in gives you feedback the way a dashboard does. Don’t wait until every warning light is on. We’ll keep improving this feature—watch the updates.
- **“I don’t need support.”** Don’t lie to yourself. You do. Burnout is common, and you’re not a special exception.
- **“A female therapist won’t get me.”** We hear you. But there’s no therapist here. It’s just you and practical exercise cards written in a straightforward, no‑nonsense voice. And yes—these were shaped with input from a qualified male psychotherapist.

## How to use it

### 1) Daily check‑in (10 seconds)
Rate your day from 1–5. That’s your “Am I okay?” for today. After a few days you’ll see a pattern—and you’ll know where you’re slipping. We’ll keep improving the insights here, and your feedback helps.

You also took a short psychological questionnaire when you started. We plan to expand it into a more detailed diagnostic tool so you can track changes over time.

### 2) The core: cards / exercises
Pick the themes that hit your real pain points. Each theme has 10 exercises you can repeat as often as you need.

Turn it into a routine. Earn points and achievements, and unlock new articles through progress.

## Why the cards work (and why doing beats “thinking about it”)
When you only think “I’m stressed,” you’re mostly running on the emotional alarm system (the amygdala). The thought loops like a stuck record.

But when you open a card and do the exercise—name the thought, write it down, run a CBT reframe, use ACT acceptance—your prefrontal cortex (the rational “control tower”) comes online.

That’s what drives neuroplasticity: new connections strengthen, old fear‑paths weaken. fMRI studies show that with regular CBT practice, amygdala reactivity tends to decrease and connectivity with the prefrontal cortex increases—so the brain starts seeing the problem less like a “catastrophe” and more like “a thing I can solve.”

ACT and mindfulness practices show a similar pattern: doing the practice (not just reading about it) shifts perception and judgment within 7–14 days.

Thinking is one thing. Training is another. It’s like the gym: reading about bench press won’t build your chest. You have to lift.

You’re not “talking to yourself.” You’re rewiring your head—privately, anonymously, with no audience. In our test group, people said their mind felt “quieter” by day 5–7.

## Privacy & anonymity

### 1) We don’t ask for personal details
Because we don’t need them—and frankly, we don’t like giving them either. You have an ID, and that’s it.

### 2) Your answers stay on your device
If you use the app on your phone, the data stays on your phone. We can’t read it—and we don’t want to.

You can verify this easily: open Menhausen on another device and your card answers will be empty.

The trade‑off: if you change phones or delete accounts, your progress can be lost. We’re exploring ways to help you keep your data without us being able to read it. We’ll tell you when we’ve got a solid solution.

### 3) What we actually see (and store)
We do see aggregated, non‑sensitive signals like:

- your ratings and feedback on cards
- the language you chose
- the country you opened the app in
- your check‑ins (to measure whether the app is helping)

And remember: you’re not “broken.” You’re a man who finally decided to fix what’s been rattling for too long.
